Location of the project expressed in decimal degrees, assumed to use the WGS84 coordinate system (as in Google Maps).
is a type of underground mining method used to extract coal from a seam that is too deep for open-pit mining
is a type of surface mining where the overlying vegetation, rock, and soil (overburden) is removed in order to reach the underlying coal seam, which usually lies a few feet beneath the surface.
is a mining practice where the tops of mountains are removed, exposing the seams of coal. It is a form of surface mining at the summit or summit ridge of a mountain.
also known as “Room and Pillar”, it’s a method of underground mining. In this method, seams of coal are mined partially, leaving large pillars of coal intact to support the overlying layers of rock
also known as “Open Cast”, refers to mining directly on the ground surface
Unique identifier assigned by the GEM's Global Energy Ownership Tracker team.
The year when the mine commenced its commercial operations.
The year in which the total coal reserves of the mine were reported or evaluated.
The year in which the percentage breakdown of coal grade was reported or deocumented.
The year in which the life of mine is reported or evaluated.
The year in which the gas-level rating of the coal mine was appraised or officially reported.
The year in which the coal mine methane emissions were reported or released.
The year in which the coal mine is scheduled to close or has already ceased operations.
The year during which the cited coal production occurred. (If coal output for the current year is unavailable, data for the most recently available year is given.)
The vertical distance from the ground surface down to the coal seam that is being mined.
The total surface area of the mining covered by the coal mining operation, including surface facilities, access roads, overburden dumps, and the underground or open-pit mine footprint.
The total number of personnel employed by a coal mining operation, including those directly involved in extraction and those supporting operations.
The total estimated volume of a coal deposit in the ground
The specific technique used to extract coal from the earth.
Starting in 2022, the Global Coal Mine Tracker adopted the latest conversion factor from the IPCC’s Sixth Assessment Report (2022), using a 20-year Global Warming Potential (GWP) of 82.5.
Starting in 2022, the Global Coal Mine Tracker adopted the latest conversion factor from the IPCC’s Sixth Assessment Report (2022), using a 100-year Global Warming Potential (GWP) of 29.8.
Some mitigation projects span multiple mines, and some mines are associated with multiple mitigation projects. See this companion dataset for more details, and methodology wiki page here.

Some mines may have multiple plumes from different dates/times of observation associated with them. See the wiki page for each GCMT asset for details and download the full Global Methane Emitters Tracker here.
Please see GEM's wiki on coal reserves for details.
Notes

In this release, this indicator is available only for coal mines in China, where mines are typically classified into three levels: Low-gas, High-gas, and Outburst. For a detailed explanation of the methodology, please visit the wiki page here.
For more details, please refer to GEM’s methodology wiki page on estimating methane emissions from coal mines.
For more details, please refer to GEM's wiki page on estimating coal mine workforce size.

Due to limited production data for Chinese coal mines, CMM emissions are estimated using the latest annual capacity utilization rate—72.8% for the coal mining and washing industry—as reported by the National Bureau of Statistics of China in 2024.
Column name changed from "Reported Coal Mine Methane Emissions (thousand tonnes  CO2e)" to "Reported Coal Mine Methane Emissions (thousand tonnes per year)"
Column name changed from "Country"  to "Country/Area"
